spec: Rank cat pictures by the expressiveness of the cat's face. Examine the cat's eyes for emotional states — the slow half-lidded droop of deep relaxation, the wide black-pupil stare of overstimulation, the hard unblinking focus of a cat tracking prey, or the soft squint of feline affection. Read the ears for signals: ears pricked forward for curiosity or alertness, ears flattened sideways ("airplane ears") for irritation or anxiety, ears pinned fully back for fear or aggression. Note whether the mouth and whiskers add expressiveness — a mid-yawn, a mid-meow, or whiskers fanned forward in engagement. Rank images high when the cat's face is clearly communicating a readable feeling such as contentment, rage, bewilderment, or imperial disdain. Rank images low when the face is neutral, closed, or offering the generic resting expression of a cat that is neither particularly engaged nor particularly bothered. The central question is whether the face is actively transmitting an inner state or is simply present.

Rank cat pictures by the expressiveness of the cat's body posture and physical tension. Assess the degree of tension or relaxation visible in the posture. A rigid arch with a puffed tail communicates alarm. A boneless sprawl draped over furniture with limbs dangling communicates deep trust and surrender. A coiled crouch signals hunting focus. A deliberate loaf — paws tucked neatly beneath the body — suggests composed serenity. Rank images high when the body posture is dynamic and communicative: stretching, leaping, rolling, hunting, arching, or flopping with theatrical abandon. Rank especially high when the body language reinforces or elaborates on what the cat's face is conveying. Rank images low when the cat is in a generic, upright, unremarkable position — sitting or standing in a default pose that any cat might hold at any time without particular intention, adding no physical story to the image.

Score how specific and idiosyncratic the cat's character appears in this single image. Assess whether the image captures something that feels particular to this one cat in this one moment, rather than a generic pose interchangeable with any other cat. Look for idiosyncrasy — a pose so peculiar, an expression so loaded with apparent intention, or a situation so specific that a viewer would instinctively narrate it: "This cat is judging me," "This cat has just committed a crime and feels no remorse," or "This cat is experiencing an existential crisis." A cat sitting in a box is ordinary. A cat wedged impossibly into a box far too small, with its face squished against the cardboard and one paw extended in defiance of physics, is specific. Score high (closer to 1) when the image could not belong to just any cat — when the combination of expression, posture, and moment creates something that feels like it reveals an individual personality. Score low (closer to 0) when the image is pleasant but anonymous, depicting a cat in a way that tells you nothing about who that particular cat is.
